text:
[Source: Blog (blog.dalanmiller.com) — "A Retrospective as a Master of Information Systems Management" (CMU Heinz MISM alumnus) | Type: student_blog]
Advice to prospective students:
On career services: "Career Services will not get you a job and it's not their job to do so...you have to find it."
On the capstone: "I was quite jealous later to hear about all the interesting technical projects offered by the 'vanilla' capstone project program from Heinz."
On balance: "it's definitely a slippery slope when you decide to 'work' every hour of your precious weekend."

[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: Penguin | Network Security | Grad year 2026 | Mar 12, 2026]
My review is that this college is very rigorous and will push you to your physical and mental limits. This college is known for making its students work long hours and the teachers know this and are very understanding as a result. The job prospects are not as good as they used to be because of the economic slow down.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: Lily | Biology | Grad year 2019 | Mar 11, 2026]
Carnegie Mellon was an academically intense environment that could sometimes feel overwhelming and competitive. While the culture was very rigorous and at times challenging, it ultimately taught me discipline, resilience, and a strong work ethic. I also had several memorable professors who were incredibly supportive mentors and made a meaningful impact on my overall experience.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: Z | Graphic Design | Grad year 2015 | Mar 11, 2026]
I attended the School of Design within Carnegie Mellon University. The School of Design has a great program split into multiple track options, with an introductory year to learn foundations applying to all tracks. I liked this system because it allowed myself and classmates to understand better how we wanted to tailor our next 3 years of education and get the most of our time there. The cost of the institution is high, and I just finished paying my student loans 10 years after graduating. I feel it was worth it to have access to great educators and companies that recruited intentionally from the school. I got my first job directly out of school because the company attended the yearly School of Design recruiting event.

[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: Paige Brady | Cognitive Psychology | Grad year 2025 | Feb 10, 2026]
I would absolutely recommend Carnegie Mellon if you know exactly what you want to do and what you expect to get out of your education. There are resources, but no one goes out of the way to inform you perse. If you want to double-major, I would recommend that instead of the BXA program. It is a very prestigious and academic school, but they are not great with interdisciplinary or innovative students.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: emonroe123 | Data Analytics | Grad year 2024 | Feb 9, 2026]
CMU CFA doesn't have any department or program, even though they advertise one. Whenever I spoke with a faculty member, they were dumbfounded by the idea of art or even more confused about the idea of students having careers or jobs. I can't stress enough that the experience has zero ROI and provides no direction or foundation for a career. There were no resources or training or access to any technology. I would have never imagined making a life decision to pursue a career just to find myself in a program that had zero interest in my success.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: biobae | Biology | Grad year 2024 | Jan 14, 2026]
I enjoyed my time at CMU and found a lot of comfort in the smaller class sizes and campus as a whole. Starting at the university in 2020 during Covid, my experience does differ from many other alumni but my junior and senior years more accurately reflected the true social and academic environments. I think the university does need to improve their class selections and hold some of the professors to higher standards of teaching. The university also seemed to be less supportive towards Greek life and other social organizations, as opposed to other extracurricular activities.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: Eric Crosby | Art & Design | Grad year 2025 | Sep 22, 2025]
Absolutely ridiculous experience. The administration has only one motivation- take advantage of the student population. My student experience was abysmal and no one cared. The faculty don't show up for courses, there are no real classes, faculty don't grad assignments or give feedback, and students don't have any access to technology or facilities to work with. Reputations come and go and CMU is an example of a palace coasting on branding with little to no ROI for students.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: collintime | Art History | Grad year 2024 | Aug 14, 2025]
This school was one of the biggest disappointments of my life. It didn't prepare me for any form of a sustainable art practice, no technical rigor, no grounding in how the art world actually works, and no emphasis on building a body of work that could survive outside the classroom bubble. The so-called "career services" were nonexistent; there was no real network to plug into, no mentorship from working artists, and no guidance on how to navigate galleries, residencies, grants, or any kind of professional opportunities. Faculty seemed more interested in protecting their own reputation than challenging students to grow. Critiques were shallow, often more about personalities than substance, and there was zero follow-through to help you develop your work beyond a semester. The school operates in a vacuum, disconnected from the realities of making a living as an artist, leaving graduates stranded with debt, a degree that carries little weight, and no practical path forward.

The school operates in a vacuum, disconnected from the realities of making a living as an artist, leaving graduates stranded with debt, a degree that carries little weight, and no practical path forward.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: Tarafalcone | Art & Design | Grad year 2025 | Jun 27, 2025]
I attended the Carnegie Mellon School of Art hoping for a progressive, interdisciplinary, and supportive environment where creativity could thrive. Unfortunately, my experience was largely the opposite. While the university's reputation in computer science and robotics is world-renowned, the School of Art feels like an afterthought—underfunded, under-supported, and structurally disconnected from the innovation happening elsewhere on campus. Most significantly, the faculty went out of their way to embarrass me and interfere in my career post-graduation, blocking me from connections in NY and interfering in my attempt to get a PhD from MIT. The curriculum is inflexible and discourages true experimentation. Despite CMU's claims of fostering interdisciplinary exploration, I found it incredibly difficult to meaningfully engage with departments outside of art. Bureaucratic hurdles and a lack of collaboration between programs made it frustrating to pursue hybrid work that crossed into technology, design, or media studies—areas in which CMU is supposedly a leader. The culture within the school can feel toxic, with a strong emphasis on cynicism and competition. Many critiques felt performative and overly academic, focusing more on jargon than on meaningful artistic growth. The environment wasn't nurturing—it often felt like a space where students were expected to prove they belonged by being constantly critical, rather than supportive or constructive. There is no training in critique or any structure to the critique environment. Compared to other programs I explored (such as RISD or UCLA), CMU's School of Art lacks the infrastructure, community, and visionary leadership to truly support emerging artists. It felt like the program was coasting on the university's name without investing in the students or adapting to the needs of contemporary creative practice.

Unfortunately, my experience was largely the opposite. While the university's reputation in computer science and robotics is world-renowned, the School of Art feels like an afterthought—underfunded, under-supported, and structurally disconnected from the innovation happening elsewhere on campus. Most significantly, the faculty went out of their way to embarrass me and interfere in my career post-graduation, blocking me from connections in NY and interfering in my attempt to get a PhD from MIT. The curriculum is inflexible and discourages true experimentation. Despite CMU's claims of fostering interdisciplinary exploration, I found it incredibly difficult to meaningfully engage with departments outside of art. Bureaucratic hurdles and a lack of collaboration between programs made it frustrating to pursue hybrid work that crossed into technology, design, or media studies—areas in which CMU is supposedly a leader. The culture within the school can feel toxic, with a strong emphasis on cynicism and competition. Many critiques felt performative and overly academic, focusing more on jargon than on meaningful artistic growth. The environment wasn't nurturing—it often felt like a space where students were expected to prove they belonged by being constantly critical, rather than supportive or constructive. There is no training in critique or any structure to the critique environment. Compared to other programs I explored (such as RISD or UCLA), CMU's School of Art lacks the infrastructure, community, and visionary leadership to truly support emerging artists. It felt like the program was coasting on the university's name without investing in the students or adapting to the needs of contemporary creative practice.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: Portlandnative | Fine Arts | Grad year 2024 | May 2, 2025]
CMU was a waste of time. I received no education and no connections whatsoever by attending there. Three years of graduate work resulted in no career advancement whatsoever. It's hard to fathom how horrifying this really is. Going to school should in the very least result in some sort of opportunity. I did really well in the program but the degree just really doesn't have any value. I am genuinely shocked and saddened to learn I made a three year long mistake that has had no impact (at most a negative one) on my career.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: AndreaRes | Fine Arts | Grad year 2024 | Jan 13, 2025]
School of Fine Arts at CMU is really not worth it. Surprised the department is still even functioning at all. When I was there the IDEATE program started and it looks like in the long run the art program can be transformed into something more useful like that program. The faculty I had to work with and the facilities we had were really really dire. But once you move and sign up it's very difficult to consider a different program. However it was not worth it at all.

text:
[Source: GradReports — Carnegie Mellon University student/alumni reviews | Type: program_review | Review: John | Information Systems | Grad year 2015 | May 1, 2019]
Carnegie Mellon is a very academically challenging school. The social life and lack of school/life balance makes it even more challenging. But, five years into my career, I feel it prepared me for success in the workforce in every way possible. I have worked at top companies in Silicon Valley and been able to rise quickly thanks to the breadth of my skills. I compare myself to colleagues my age from other leading universities like Stanford and continue to feel I excel above them in many ways due to the work ethic CMU instilled in me. One of the biggest things I took away from CMU was learning how to learn - picking up a new skill, software language, etc. and being able to experiment and learn on my own. CMU also is a well respected name in the technology industry and you won't have trouble getting a job if it's on your resume. For current and prospective students, I would encourage you to get involved with the community of Pittsburgh and possibly in Greek life if you are struggling. Having some activities outside of school helped me in my later years of undergrad, just to destress. Pittsburgh also has some cool restaurants, shows, organizations etc if you venture off campus. Don't stay in the CMU bubble the whole time!

Having some activities outside of school helped me in my later years of undergrad, just to destress. Pittsburgh also has some cool restaurants, shows, organizations etc if you venture off campus. Don't stay in the CMU bubble the whole time!

text:
[Source: Niche — Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy reviews | Type: program_review | Review: 5 stars | 2026 | Master of Public Management]
The Master of Public Management program at Carnegie Mellon University's Heinz College is a rigorous and rewarding experience for working professionals. The program attracts a diverse cohort of students from government, nonprofit, and private sectors which creates a uniquely rich learning environment. The curriculum is challenging and highly relevant to real world policy and management problems. As someone who works simultaneously in nonprofit education finance and tax policy, I find the program directly applicable to the work I do every day. The part time structure allows students like me to maintain careers while earning a graduate degree from one of the top ranked universities in the country.

The program attracts a diverse cohort of students from government, nonprofit, and private sectors which creates a uniquely rich learning environment. The curriculum is challenging and highly relevant to real world policy and management problems. As someone who works simultaneously in nonprofit education finance and tax policy, I find the program directly applicable to the work I do every day. The part time structure allows students like me to maintain careers while earning a graduate degree from one of the top ranked universities in the country.

text:
[Source: Niche — Heinz College of Information Systems and Public Policy reviews | Type: program_review | Review: 5 stars | 2020]
Heinz College has many "Minis" to choose from. These are half semester courses that allow you to do a quick and fairly deep dive into specific subjects. It is useful because you get exposure to a lot of different subjects through the duration of your program, but it can be hard too. Since you only have several weeks to learn everything, the pace is quick. The core courses create a solid base for the program. There are tons of electives to branch out into your sub-specialty. Also, you are typically able to take classes at the computer science school or business school, which provides further flexibility.
raw_text: Heinz College has many "Minis" to choose from. These are half semester courses that allow you to do a quick and fairly deep dive into specific subjects. It is useful because you get exposure to a lot of different subjects through the duration of your program, but it can be hard too. Since you only have several weeks to learn everything, the pace is quick. The core courses create a solid base for the program. There are tons of electives to branch out into your sub-specialty. Also, you are typically able to take classes at the computer science school or business school, which provides further flexibility.

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Carnegie Mellon's Heinz College MISM curriculum pairs well with selective CS courses that add technical depth, systems understanding, and machine-learning/data-skills useful for product, analytics, and technology-management roles. Below are recommended CS courses grouped by career goals, with brief rationale and what to expect from each.
raw_text: Carnegie Mellon's Heinz College MISM curriculum pairs well with selective CS courses that add technical depth, systems understanding, and machine-learning/data-skills useful for product, analytics, and technology-management roles. Below are recommended CS courses grouped by career goals, with brief rationale and what to expect from each.

metadata.source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?"

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Core CS courses (highly recommended)
15-110/15-112 Intro to Programming (if you lack programming): essential foundation in Python/C; builds problem solving and coding fluency.
15-213/15-215 Data Structures and Algorithms: fundamental algorithms, complexity, practical data structures — invaluable for technical credibility, interviews, and any engineering-facing role.
15-211/15-214 Computer Organization or 18-240 (if deeper systems): basic systems understanding (memory, CPU, assembly) that helps when managing engineering teams or designing efficient products.
raw_text:
Core CS courses (highly recommended)
15-110/15-112 Intro to Programming (if you lack programming): essential foundation in Python/C; builds problem solving and coding fluency.
15-213/15-215 Data Structures and Algorithms: fundamental algorithms, complexity, practical data structures — invaluable for technical credibility, interviews, and any engineering-facing role.
15-211/15-214 Computer Organization or 18-240 (if deeper systems): basic systems understanding (memory, CPU, assembly) that helps when managing engineering teams or designing efficient products.

metadata.source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?"

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Data science & machine learning (for analytics/product roles)
10-601/11-411 Introduction to Machine Learning / 10-701 (grad): core ML models, regularization, evaluation — critical for leading ML projects or interpreting models.
10-601 alternatives: 10-601/10-701 selection depends on math background; 10-701 is more theoretical.
36-401/36-601 Database Systems: query languages, indexing, transactions — important for data architecture and product features that rely on data access.
11-411/11-613 Advanced Machine Learning / Deep Learning electives: for deeper applied ML and neural nets used in product ML pipelines.
36-401 -> 36-615 Big Data Systems (if available): covers distributed data processing (Spark/Hadoop), useful for scaling analytics.
raw_text:
Data science & machine learning (for analytics/product roles)
10-601/11-411 Introduction to Machine Learning / 10-701 (grad): core ML models, regularization, evaluation — critical for leading ML projects or interpreting models.
10-601 alternatives: 10-601/10-701 selection depends on math background; 10-701 is more theoretical.
36-401/36-601 Database Systems: query languages, indexing, transactions — important for data architecture and product features that rely on data access.
11-411/11-613 Advanced Machine Learning / Deep Learning electives: for deeper applied ML and neural nets used in product ML pipelines.
36-401 -> 36-615 Big Data Systems (if available): covers distributed data processing (Spark/Hadoop), useful for scaling analytics.

metadata.source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?"

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Software engineering & systems (for product/engineering management)
15-213 (again) + 15-410/15-440 Software Engineering / Database-backed apps: covers software design, testing, and team workflows — directly applicable to product development.
15-410 Software Engineering: design patterns, architecture, team processes.
15-441/15-445 Computer Networks / Distributed Systems: essential for SaaS, cloud products, microservices, reliability and security trade-offs.
15-412/15-415 Operating Systems or Distributed Systems: deep dive into concurrency, fault tolerance — valuable when managing infrastructure or platform teams.

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Security, privacy, and policy (for governance/tech-policy roles)
15-440/15-441 Computer and Network Security: threat models, cryptography basics, secure system design — necessary for product privacy and governance decisions.
17-600/17-780 Information Security electives at Heinz + CS security cross-listings: combine policy and technical security context.

Security, privacy, and policy (for governance/tech-policy roles)

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Human-centered & applied AI (for product/UX/analytics)
05-381/05-482 Human-Computer Interaction / Design: product-focused design and evaluation methods to bridge technical teams and users.
11-411/11-614 Applied ML for Product: courses that emphasize deployment, A/B testing, and interpretability.

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Practical applied courses and project-based classes (highest ROI)
Capstone / Project courses (any CS project or interdisciplinary capstone): real projects with engineers; best way to demonstrate technical leadership and ability to deliver.
15-315/15-319 Software projects / Mobile/Web app courses: productization, APIs, front/back integration — immediately useful on resumes.

metadata.source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?"

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Selection strategy for a MISM student
Prioritize one core programming + one data/ML course in your first year to build technical fluency.
Choose at least one systems or software-engineering course to understand constraints engineers face.
Add one applied/project-based course to produce a portfolio artifact.
Balance course difficulty with program load — many upper-level CS courses assume CS prerequisites and significant time.
raw_text:
Selection strategy for a MISM student
Prioritize one core programming + one data/ML course in your first year to build technical fluency.
Choose at least one systems or software-engineering course to understand constraints engineers face.
Add one applied/project-based course to produce a portfolio artifact.
Balance course difficulty with program load — many upper-level CS courses assume CS prerequisites and significant time.

metadata.source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?"

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Typical course pathways (examples)
Data/Analytics track: 15-112 → 36-401 → 10-601 → Big Data or ML elective → project course.
Product/Engineering management track: 15-112 → 15-213 → 15-410 → 15-441 or 15-412 → project course.
Security/Policy track: 15-112 → 15-213 → 15-440 → Heinz security/policy electives → applied capstone.
raw_text:
Typical course pathways (examples)
Data/Analytics track: 15-112 → 36-401 → 10-601 → Big Data or ML elective → project course.
Product/Engineering management track: 15-112 → 15-213 → 15-410 → 15-441 or 15-412 → project course.
Security/Policy track: 15-112 → 15-213 → 15-440 → Heinz security/policy electives → applied capstone.

metadata.source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?"

text:
[Source: Quora — "What are the best CS courses at CMU for someone in the Heinz College's MISM program?" | Type: forum_thread]
Administrative and logistical notes
Check prerequisites and coordinate with Heinz/CS advisors for overrides; many CS classes require prior CS coursework.
Audit or take with reduced workload when possible; some CS classes are intensely time-consuming.
Look for cross-listed or CMU Progress courses designed for non-CS students (these may waive some prerequisites and teach the same core material with slightly different pace).
raw_text:

She also wouldn't let the TA really help otherwise it was considered 'them writing the paper' which left many of us with comments that weren't helpful and then our grades suffered. Other than that she was nice.

No one at Carnegie Mellon would take her courses if they were electives, so she needs to know her place and might as well let everyone pass instead of being excessively strict.

raw_text: She thinks she is an objective grader, but she's not. She is a typical pretentious bozo with a bunch of old school grading criteria. She is clearly giving some people Bs and Cs just because her ego wouldn't allow her to give everyone an A. Take the other professor Hyatt for the Heinz College cores. Avoid Stacy.

metadata.source: RateMyProfessors — Stacy Rosenberg (CMU Heinz College, Writing / Policy core)

text:
[Source: RateMyProfessors — Raja Sooriamurthi (CMU Heinz College, Information Systems) | Type: professor_review | Overall quality: 4.8/5 | Difficulty: 2.3/5 | Would take again: 100% | Review: Course 67262 | Apr 20, 2023 | Quality 5.0 | Difficulty 3.0]
My man Raja. Words can't describe my appreciation of the GOAT of CMU's IS department. There might be one or two times where you disagree with his grading, but having taken 67-262 and 67-364 with Raja, I can certify that he truly cares about his students' success and a good course experience (unlike another professor for a sophomore-level IS class).
